name: Testing Framework Selection
description: Choose the right Solana testing approach for your project

Anchor program (Rust + IDL).question: Do you need to test against real mainnet state (forked data)?

Yes — need real accounts, real token bal.rationale: Surfpool forks mainnet state and runs a local validator with real account data. Test your program against production state without paying fees.
Yes — need real accounts, real token bal.when: DeFi protocols, programs that CPI into existing programs (Jupiter, Orca, etc.), anything that reads mainnet accounts.
Yes — need real accounts, real token bal.tradeoffs: Slower startup than LiteSVM. Requires network access to fork state.

No — isolated unit tests are fine.rationale: Standard `anchor test` with local validator. The default for most Anchor projects. Well-documented, stable.
No — isolated unit tests are fine.install: Comes with Anchor. No extra install needed.
No — isolated unit tests are fine.run:
anchor test
# Or: anchor test --skip-deploy (if program already deployed)
No — isolated unit tests are fine.when: Most Anchor programs. Unit tests, integration tests, basic CPI tests.
No — isolated unit tests are fine.tradeoffs: Uses solana-test-validator which is slower than LiteSVM. No mainnet state.

Native Solana program (no Anchor).question: Do you need full transaction simulation or just instruction-level testing?

Full transaction simulation.rationale: LiteSVM is a lightweight SVM simulator. Runs in-process (no external validator). ~100x faster than solana-test-validator for unit tests.
Full transaction simulation.run: Write Rust tests using LiteSVM::new(). Call process_transaction() directly.
Full transaction simulation.when: Native programs, performance-critical test suites, CI pipelines.
Full transaction simulation.tradeoffs: No RPC interface — tests must use the Rust API. Not all syscalls are implemented.

Just test individual instructions.rationale: Mollusk tests individual Solana instructions without a full validator. Fastest option for instruction-level unit tests.
Just test individual instructions.run: Use Mollusk::new() to create a test environment. Call process_instruction() per test.
Just test individual instructions.when: Testing single instructions in isolation. Fastest feedback loop.
Just test individual instructions.tradeoffs: Can't test multi-instruction transactions or CPI chains.

Frontend / dApp (TypeScript).rationale: LiteSVM provides a fast, lightweight SVM simulator with TypeScript bindings. Replaces the deprecated solana-bankrun package.
Frontend / dApp (TypeScript).run:
import { LiteSVM } from 'litesvm';
const svm = new LiteSVM();
Frontend / dApp (TypeScript).when: TypeScript tests, frontend integration tests, testing with @solana/web3.js or @solana/kit.
Frontend / dApp (TypeScript).tradeoffs: Newer ecosystem — check docs for latest API.

Fuzz testing (find edge cases automatica.rationale: Trident is the Anchor fuzz testing framework. Generates random inputs to find crashes, panics, and unexpected behavior.
Fuzz testing (find edge cases automatica.when: Before mainnet deployment. Especially for programs handling user funds.
Fuzz testing (find edge cases automatica.tradeoffs: Slow (runs thousands of iterations). May find false positives. Requires Anchor.

Security / Formal verification.rationale: QEDGen generates formal proofs for Solana programs using Lean 4. Mathematically proves properties hold (no fuzzing randomness).
Security / Formal verification.when: High-value programs (>$1M TVL). Regulatory requirements. After fuzz testing finds no more bugs.
Security / Formal verification.tradeoffs: Steep learning curve. Requires Lean 4. Slower than fuzz testing but provides mathematical guarantees.
